ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

Downstream writers will use the query_postgres tool to inspect source data while implementing your plan.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 5, 23, 51, 453629, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

Columns:
- `kontakt_id` text NOT NULL
- `rufname` text NULL
- `familienname` text NULL
- `kontakt_email` text NULL
- `tel` text NULL
- `berufsbezeichnung` text NULL
- `rolle` text NULL
- `korrespondenzsprache` text NULL
- `kd_nummer` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden  (210 rows)

Columns:
- `kundennummer` text NOT NULL
- `unternehmensname` text NULL
- `erp_nr` text NULL
- `kundenklasse` text NULL
- `vertriebsgebiet` text NULL
- `industrie` text NULL
- `homepage` text NULL
- `stadt` text NULL
- `land_region` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities  (540 rows)

Columns:
- `opp_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `titel` text NULL
- `vertriebsphase` text NULL
- `zieldatum` text NULL
- `auftragswert` text NULL
- `waehrungscode` text NULL
- `kunden_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_projekte  (317 rows)

Columns:
- `projekt_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `projektname` text NULL
- `projektstatus` text NULL
- `go_live_datum` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `opp_kennung_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 5, 23, 51, 453635, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 5, 23, 51, 453854, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f402e-b81c-703c-a5fb-af4ba5466f6f', conversation_id='019f402e-b81c-703c-a5fb-af4a94a086e8')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 5, 25, 29, 83565, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Account`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

Columns:
- `kontakt_id` text NOT NULL
- `rufname` text NULL
- `familienname` text NULL
- `kontakt_email` text NULL
- `tel` text NULL
- `berufsbezeichnung` text NULL
- `rolle` text NULL
- `korrespondenzsprache` text NULL
- `kd_nummer` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden  (210 rows)

Columns:
- `kundennummer` text NOT NULL
- `unternehmensname` text NULL
- `erp_nr` text NULL
- `kundenklasse` text NULL
- `vertriebsgebiet` text NULL
- `industrie` text NULL
- `homepage` text NULL
- `stadt` text NULL
- `land_region` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities  (540 rows)

Columns:
- `opp_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `titel` text NULL
- `vertriebsphase` text NULL
- `zieldatum` text NULL
- `auftragswert` text NULL
- `waehrungscode` text NULL
- `kunden_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_projekte  (317 rows)

Columns:
- `projekt_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `projektname` text NULL
- `projektstatus` text NULL
- `go_live_datum` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `opp_kennung_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Source tables: fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden
- Id <- kundennummer (Use a deterministic hash (e.g., MD5) of `kundennummer` to generate a Salesforce-style `Id`. Ensure consistency across all tables referencing `AccountId`.)
- Name <- unternehmensname (Trim and use `INITCAP` to normalize case.)
- ERP_Number__c <- erp_nr (Trim and pass through as-is.)
- Customer_Tier__c <- kundenklasse (Map source values to enum: 'Gold', 'Silver', 'Bronze', 'Platinum'. Normalize case and spelling. Use NULL for unparseable values.)
- Region__c <- vertriebsgebiet (Trim and pass through as-is.)
- Industry <- industrie (Trim and use `INITCAP` to normalize case.)
- Website <- homepage (Trim and ensure it starts with 'http://' or 'https://' if not empty.)
- BillingCity <- stadt (Trim and use `INITCAP` to normalize case.)
- BillingCountry <- land_region (Trim and use `INITCAP` to normalize case.)
- Legacy_Customer_ID__c <- kundennummer (Directly map from `kundennummer`.)
- CreatedDate <- (unspecified) (Use a static default timestamp (e.g., '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z') for all rows.)
- LastModifiedDate <- (unspecified) (Use a static default timestamp (e.g., '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z') for all rows.)
- IsDeleted <- (unspecified) (Default to 0 (false) for all rows.)
Notes: The `Id` column must be a deterministic hash of `kundennummer` to ensure consistency across all tables referencing `AccountId`.

Follow the plan. You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 5, 27, 15, 718333, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Opportunity`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL

# Target schema

## Opportunity
Columns:

- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text

- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Source tables: fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities
Join keys: kunden_ref = kunden.kundennummer
- Id <- opp_kennung (Use a deterministic hash (e.g., MD5) of `opp_kennung` to generate a Salesforce-style `Id`.)
- Name <- titel (Trim and use `INITCAP` to normalize case.)
- StageName <- vertriebsphase (Map source values to enum: 'Prospecting', 'Qualification', 'Needs Analysis', 'Value Proposition', 'Id. Decision Makers', 'Perception Analysis', 'Proposal/Price Quote', 'Negotiation/Review', 'Closed Won', 'Closed Lost'. Normalize case and spelling. Use NULL for unparseable values.)
- CloseDate <- zieldatum (Parse `zieldatum` as a date (handle formats like `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`) and output as ISO `YYYY-MM-DD`. Use NULL for unparseable values.)
- Amount <- auftragswert (Strip currency symbols and text prefixes. Handle locale-specific separators (e.g., European `1.234,56` -> `1234.56`). Cast to `DOUBLE PRECISION`.)
- CurrencyIsoCode <- waehrungscode (Trim and pass through as-is.)
- AccountId <- kunden_ref (Join with `master_kunden.kundennummer` to resolve the `AccountId` (hash of `kundennummer`).)
- Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c <- opp_kennung (Directly map from `opp_kennung`.)
- CreatedDate <- (unspecified) (Use a static default timestamp (e.g., '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z') for all rows.)
- LastModifiedDate <- (unspecified) (Use a static default timestamp (e.g., '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z') for all rows.)
- IsDeleted <- (unspecified) (Default to 0 (false) for all rows.)
Notes: Join `master_opportunities` with `master_kunden` using `kunden_ref = kundennummer` to resolve `AccountId`.

Follow the plan. ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content='The join between `master_opportunities.kunden_ref` and `master_kunden.kundennummer` is **not direct**:
- `kunden_ref` values are like `KD-M1001`, `KD-M1002`, etc.
- `kundennummer` values are like `CUST-M1001`, `CUST-M1002`, etc.

This suggests a **prefix mismatch**: `KD-` vs `CUST-`. To resolve `AccountId`, we need to **map `kunden_ref` to `kundennummer`** by replacing the prefix. For example:
- `KD-M1001` → `CUST-M1001`
- `KD-M9724` → `CUST-M9724`

I will verify this mapping by checking if all `kunden_ref` values in `master_opportunities` have a corresponding `kundennummer` in `master_kunden` after replacing `KD-` with `CUST-`.'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='query_postgres', args='{"sql": "SELECT DISTINCT mo.kunden_ref, mk.kundennummer \nFROM \"fixture_master_v2_src\".\"master_opportunities\" mo \nLEFT JOIN \"fixture_master_v2_src\".\"master_kunden\" mk ON REPLACE(mo.kunden_ref, 'KD-', 'CUST-') = mk.kundennummer \nWHERE mo.kunden_ref IS NOT NULL \nLIMIT 20"}', tool_call_id='vlWWkg8HQ')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=4878, output_tokens=327), model_name='nvidia/mistral-medium-3.5-128b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 5, 27, 58, 767067, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 5, 27, 40, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-afdd96f440194981', finish_reason='tool_call')

args.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT mo.kunden_ref, mk.kundennummer 
FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_opportunities" mo 
LEFT JOIN "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_kunden" mk ON REPLACE(mo.kunden_ref, 'KD-', 'CUST-') = mk.kundennummer 
WHERE mo.kunden_ref IS NOT NULL 
LIMIT 20

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.

- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

Decision Makers', 'Perception Analysis', 'Proposal/Price Quote', 'Negotiation/Review', 'Closed Won', 'Closed Lost'. Normalize case and spelling. Use NULL for unparseable values.)

- CurrencyIsoCode <- waehrungscode (Trim and pass through as-is.)
- AccountId <- kunden_ref (Join with `master_kunden.kundennummer` to resolve the `AccountId` (hash of `kundennummer`).)
- Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c <- opp_kennung (Directly map from `opp_kennung`.)
- CreatedDate <- (unspecified) (Use a static default timestamp (e.g., '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z') for all rows.)
- LastModifiedDate <- (unspecified) (Use a static default timestamp (e.g., '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z') for all rows.)
- IsDeleted <- (unspecified) (Default to 0 (false) for all rows.)
Notes: Join `master_opportunities` with `master_kunden` using `kunden_ref = kundennummer` to resolve `AccountId`.

Follow the plan. - **`AccountId`**: MD5 hash of `CUST-<numeric_part>` (derived from `kunden_ref` by replacing `KD-` with `CUST-` and joining with `master_kunden`).
- **`Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c`**: Directly map `opp_kennung`.
- **`CreatedDate`** and **`LastModifiedDate`**: Static default `'2023-01-01T00:00:00Z'`.
- **`IsDeleted`**: Default to `0`.

Here is the model:"), ToolCallPart(tool_name='final_result', args='{"target_table": "Opportunity", "dbt_sql": "{{ config(materialized='table') }}\n\nWITH opportunity_data AS (\n    SELECT\n        mo.opp_kennung,\n        mo.titel,\n        mo.vertriebsphase,\n        mo.zieldatum,\n        mo.auftragswert,\n        mo.waehrungscode,\n        mo.kunden_ref,\n        mk.kundennummer\n    FROM {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} mo\n    LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} mk\n        ON REPLACE(mo.kunden_ref, 'KD-', 'CUST-') = mk.kundennummer\n)\n\nSELECT\n    MD5(od.opp_kennung) AS \"Id\",\n    INITCAP(TRIM(od.titel)) AS \"Name\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('needs analysis', 'bedarfsanalyse') THEN 'Needs Analysis'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('value proposition', 'wertargumentation') THEN 'Value Proposition'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('id. decision makers', 'entscheider identifiziert') THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('perception analysis', 'wahrnehmungsanalyse') THEN 'Perception Analysis'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'angebot') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('negotiation/review', 'verhandlung') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)', 'closed won') THEN 'Closed Won'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)', 'lost') THEN 'Closed Lost'\n        ELSE NULL\n    END AS \"StageName\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN od.zieldatum ~ '^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}$' THEN od.zieldatum\n        WHEN od.zieldatum ~ '^\\d{2}\\.\\d{2}\\.\\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(od.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')\n        WHEN od.zieldatum ~ '^\\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(od.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')\n        WHEN od.zieldatum ~ '^\\d{1,2}/\\d{1,2}/\\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(od.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')\n        ELSE NULL\n    END AS \"CloseDate\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN od.auftragswert IS NULL OR TRIM(od.auftragswert) = 'None' THEN NULL\n        WHEN od.auftragswert ~ '^[0-9]+\\.[0-9]{3},[0-9]{2}$' THEN \n            CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE(od.auftragswert, '.', ''), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)\n        WHEN od.auftragswert ~ '^[0-9]+,[0-9]{2}$' THEN \n            CAST(REPLACE(od.auftragswert, ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)\n        WHEN od.auftragswert ~ '^[0-9]+\\.[0-9]{2}$' THEN \n            CAST(od.auftragswert AS DOUBLE PRECISION)\n        WHEN od.auftragswert ~ '^[0-9]+$' THEN \n            CAST(od.auftragswert AS DOUBLE PRECISION)\n        WHEN od.auftragswert ~ '^-?[0-9]+\\.[0-9]{2}$' THEN \n            CAST(od.auftragswert AS DOUBLE PRECISION)\n        ELSE \n            CASE\n                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(od.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') ~ '^-?[0-9]+\\.[0-9]{3},[0-9]{2}$' THEN \n                    CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(od.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g'), '.', ''), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)\n                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(od.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') ~ '^-?[0-9]+,[0-9]{2}$' THEN \n                    CAST(REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(od.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g'), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)\n                ELSE NULL\n            END\n    END AS \"Amount\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN od.waehrungscode IS NULL THEN NULL\n        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(od.waehrungscode, '[^A-Za-z]', '', 'g')))\n    END AS \"CurrencyIsoCode\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN od.kundennummer IS NOT NULL THEN MD5(od.kundennummer)\n        ELSE NULL\n    END AS \"AccountId\",\n    od.opp_kennung AS \"Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c\",\n    '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z' AS \"CreatedDate\",\n    '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z' AS \"LastModifiedDate\",\n    0 AS \"IsDeleted\"\nFROM opportunity_data od", "column_notes": [{"target_column": "Id", "source_columns": ["master_opportunities.opp_kennung"], "explanation": "MD5 hash of the source opportunity key `opp_kennung` to generate a Salesforce-style Id."}, {"target_column": "Name", "source_columns": ["master_opportunities.titel"], "explanation": "Trimmed and title-cased version of `titel`."}, {"target_column": "StageName", "source_columns": ["master_opportunities.vertriebsphase"], "explanation": "Mapped from `vertriebsphase` to the target enum values (case-insensitive). NULL for unparseable."}, {"target_column": "Amount", "source_columns": ["master_opportunities.auftragswert"], "explanation": "Stripped of currency symbols and locale-specific separators (e.g., EUR 1.234,56 → 1234.56). ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`

1. The `kunden_kennung` in `master_projekte` matches `kundennummer` in `master_kunden` for valid customers.
2. The `opp_kennung_ref` in `master_projekte` matches `opp_kennung` in `master_opportunities` for valid opportunities.

I will verify these joins and inspect the data for edge cases.'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='query_postgres', args='{"sql": "SELECT \n    mp.\"projekt_kennung\", \n    mp.\"kunden_kennung\", \n    mk.\"kundennummer\", \n    mp.\"opp_kennung_ref\", \n    mo.\"opp_kennung\"\nFROM \"fixture_master_v2_src\".\"master_projekte\" mp \nLEFT JOIN \"fixture_master_v2_src\".\"master_kunden\" mk ON mp.\"kunden_kennung\" = mk.\"kundennummer\"\nLEFT JOIN \"fixture_master_v2_src\".\"master_opportunities\" mo ON mp.\"opp_kennung_ref\" = mo.\"opp_kennung\"\nWHERE mp.\"kunden_kennung\" IS NULL OR mp.\"opp_kennung_ref\" IS NULL OR mk.\"kundennummer\" IS NULL OR mo.\"opp_kennung\" IS NULL\nLIMIT 20"}', tool_call_id='ZzYeN5SPE')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=6315, output_tokens=304), model_name='nvidia/mistral-medium-3.5-128b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 5, 30, 4, 329637, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 5, 29, 23, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-9a8993afd3f90406', finish_reason='tool_call')

args.sql:
SELECT 
    mp."projekt_kennung", 
    mp."kunden_kennung", 
    mk."kundennummer", 
    mp."opp_kennung_ref", 
    mo."opp_kennung"
FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_projekte" mp 
LEFT JOIN "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_kunden" mk ON mp."kunden_kennung" = mk."kundennummer"
LEFT JOIN "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_opportunities" mo ON mp."opp_kennung_ref" = mo."opp_kennung"
WHERE mp."kunden_kennung" IS NULL OR mp."opp_kennung_ref" IS NULL OR mk."kundennummer" IS NULL OR mo."opp_kennung" IS

messages.raw:
ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 5, 29, 0, 422703, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Project__c`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden  (210 rows)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities  (540 rows)

Columns:

- `titel` text NULL
- `vertriebsphase` text NULL
- `zieldatum` text NULL
- `auftragswert` text NULL
- `waehrungscode` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_projekte  (317 rows)

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text

## Opportunity

## Project__c
Columns:

- `Go_Live_Date__c` text

- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text

- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Approved mapping plan
Source tables: fixture_master_v2_src.master_projekte
Join keys: kunden_kennung = kunden.kundennummer, opp_kennung_ref = opportunities.opp_kennung
- Id <- projekt_kennung (Use a deterministic hash (e.g., MD5) of `projekt_kennung` to generate a Salesforce-style `Id`.)
- Name <- projektname (Trim and use `INITCAP` to normalize case.)
- Project_Status__c <- projektstatus (Map source values to enum: 'Active', 'Completed', 'In Planning', 'On Hold', 'Cancelled'. Normalize case and spelling. Use NULL for unparseable values.)
- Go_Live_Date__c <- go_live_datum (Parse `go_live_datum` as a date (handle formats like `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`) and output as ISO `YYYY-MM-DD`. Use NULL for unparseable values.)
- Account__c <- kunden_kennung (Join with `master_kunden.kundennummer` to resolve the `Account__c` (hash of `kundennummer`).)
- Opportunity__c <- opp_kennung_ref (Join with `master_opportunities.opp_kennung` to resolve the `Opportunity__c` (hash of `opp_kennung`).)

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content='I now have enough information to construct the `Project__c` model. Key observations:
- `projekt_kennung` is the natural key for projects.
- `kunden_kennung` in `master_projekte` matches `kundennummer` in `master_kunden` for valid customers, but some projects have `MISSING-*` values (no matching customer).
- `opp_kennung_ref` in `master_projekte` matches `opp_kennung` in `master_opportunities` for valid opportunities, but some projects reference non-existent opportunities (e.g., `OPP-M-99332`).
- `go_live_datum` has formats like `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`, and invalid values like `0000-00-00`.
- `projektstatus` values include `Abgeschlossen`, `active`, `In Planung`, `Cancelled`, `Planung`, `On Hold`.

I will:
1. Generate `Id` as `MD5(projekt_kennung)`.
2. Normalize `Name` with `INITCAP(TRIM(projektname))`.
3. Map `projektstatus` to the enum values: `Active`, `Completed`, `In Planning`, `On Hold`, `Cancelled`.
4. Parse `go_live_datum` with a multi-format approach, defaulting to `NULL` for unparseable values.
5. Resolve `Account__c` as `MD5(kundennummer)` from the joined `master_kunden` table.
6. Resolve `Opportunity__c` as `MD5(opp_kennung)` from the joined `master_opportunities` table.
7. Use static defaults for `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, and `IsDeleted`.

Now I will write the dbt model.'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='final_result', args='{"target_table": "Project__c", "dbt_sql": "{{ config(materialized='table') }}\n\nWITH project_data AS (\n    SELECT\n        mp.\"projekt_kennung\",\n        mp.\"projektname\",\n        mp.\"projektstatus\",\n        mp.\"go_live_datum\",\n        mp.\"kunden_kennung\",\n        mp.\"opp_kennung_ref\",\n        mk.\"kundennummer\" AS account_kundennummer,\n        mo.\"opp_kennung\" AS opportunity_opp_kennung\n    FROM {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_projekte') }} mp\n    LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} mk\n        ON mp.\"kunden_kennung\" = mk.\"kundennummer\"\n    LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} mo\n        ON mp.\"opp_kennung_ref\" = mo.\"opp_kennung\"\n)\n\nSELECT\n    MD5(p.\"projekt_kennung\") AS \"Id\",\n    INITCAP(TRIM(p.\"projektname\")) AS \"Name\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.\"projektstatus\")) IN ('ACTIVE', 'AKTIV') THEN 'Active'\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.\"projektstatus\")) IN ('ABGESCHLOSSEN', 'COMPLETED') THEN 'Completed'\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.\"projektstatus\")) IN ('IN PLANUNG', 'PLANUNG', 'IN PLANNING') THEN 'In Planning'\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.\"projektstatus\")) IN ('ON HOLD', 'HOLD') THEN 'On Hold'\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.\"projektstatus\")) IN ('CANCELLED', 'STORNIERT') THEN 'Cancelled'\n        ELSE NULL\n    END AS \"Project_Status__c\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN TRIM(p.\"go_live_datum\") ~ '^\\d{4}\\d{2}\\d{2}$' THEN\n            TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.\"go_live_datum\", 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')\n        WHEN TRIM(p.\"go_live_datum\") ~ '^\\d{1,2}/\\d{1,2}/\\d{4}$' THEN\n            TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.\"go_live_datum\", 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')\n        WHEN TRIM(p.\"go_live_datum\") ~ '^\\d{1,2}\\.\\d{1,2}\\.\\d{4}$' THEN\n            TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.\"go_live_datum\", 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')\n        WHEN TRIM(p.\"go_live_datum\") ~ '^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}$' AND p.\"go_live_datum\" != '0000-00-00' THEN\n            p.\"go_live_datum\"\n        ELSE NULL\n    END AS \"Go_Live_Date__c\",\n    CASE WHEN p.account_kundennummer IS NOT NULL THEN MD5(p.account_kundennummer) ELSE NULL END AS \"Account__c\",\n    CASE WHEN p.opportunity_opp_kennung IS NOT NULL THEN MD5(p.opportunity_opp_kennung) ELSE NULL END AS \"Opportunity__c\",\n    p.\"projekt_kennung\" AS \"Legacy_Project_ID__c\",\n    '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z' AS \"CreatedDate\",\n    '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z' AS \"LastModifiedDate\",\n    0 AS \"IsDeleted\"\nFROM project_data p", "column_notes": [{"target_column": "Id", "source_columns": ["master_projekte.projekt_kennung"], "explanation": "Deterministic hash (MD5) of the source natural key `projekt_kennung`."}, {"target_column": "Name", "source_columns": ["master_projekte.projektname"], "explanation": "Normalized with INITCAP and TRIM for consistent casing."}, {"target_column": "Project_Status__c", "source_columns": ["master_projekte.projektstatus"], "explanation": "Mapped from source `projektstatus` to the enum - target_table: name of the model

- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.

- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Use the query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect the OUTPUT of the dbt models by querying the output schema
3. Verify data quality: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
4. Check for cross-table consistency issues

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise (cite specific data issues found via queries)

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.

# Proposed mapping

## Account
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(kundennummer) AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(COALESCE(unternehmensname, ''))) AS "Name",
    TRIM(erp_nr) AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(kundenklasse)) IN ('gold', 'golden') THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(kundenklasse)) IN ('silver', 'silber') THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(kundenklasse)) IN ('bronze') THEN 'Bronze'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(kundenklasse)) IN ('platinum') THEN 'Platinum'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    TRIM(vertriebsgebiet) AS "Region__c",
    INITCAP(TRIM(COALESCE(industrie, ''))) AS "Industry",
    CASE
        WHEN homepage IS NOT NULL AND homepage !~ '^https?://' THEN 'http://' || TRIM(homepage)
        ELSE TRIM(homepage)
    END AS "Website",
```

```sql
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(k.rolle)) IN ('END USER', 'ENDANWENDER', 'END USER') THEN 'End User'

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(k.rolle)) IN ('EXECUTIVE SPONSOR') THEN 'Executive Sponsor'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Role__c",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(k.korrespondenzsprache)) IN ('DEUTSCH', 'DE', 'GERMAN') THEN 'DE'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(k.korrespondenzsprache)) IN ('ENGLISH', 'ENGLISCH', 'EN') THEN 'EN'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(k.korrespondenzsprache)) IN ('FRANZÖSISCH', 'FRENCH', 'FR') THEN 'FR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(k.korrespondenzsprache)) IN ('SPANISCH', 'SPANISH', 'ES') THEN 'ES'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(k.korrespondenzsprache)) IN ('ITALIENISCH', 'ITALIAN', 'IT') THEN 'IT'
        ELSE NULL
```

```sql
        WHEN garantieende ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN 
            TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(garantieende, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN garantieende ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN

FROM asset_data
```

## Opportunity
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

WITH opportunity_data AS (
    SELECT
        mo.opp_kennung,
        mo.titel,
        mo.vertriebsphase,
        mo.zieldatum,
        mo.auftragswert,
        mo.waehrungscode,
        mo.kunden_ref,
        mk.kundennummer
    FROM {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} mo
    LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} mk
        ON REPLACE(mo.kunden_ref, 'KD-', 'CUST-') = mk.kundennummer
)

SELECT
    MD5(od.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(od.titel)) AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('needs analysis', 'bedarfsanalyse') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('value proposition', 'wertargumentation') THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('id. decision makers', 'entscheider identifiziert') THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('perception analysis', 'wahrnehmungsanalyse') THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'angebot') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('negotiation/review', 'verhandlung') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)', 'closed won') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(od.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)', 'lost') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE
        WHEN od.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN od.zieldatum
        WHEN od.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(od.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN od.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(od.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN od.zieldatum ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(od.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN od.auftragswert IS NULL OR TRIM(od.auftragswert) = 'None' THEN NULL
        WHEN od.auftragswert ~ '^[0-9]+\.[0-9]{3},[0-9]{2}$' THEN 
            CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE(od.auftragswert, '.', ''), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN od.auftragswert ~ '^[0-9]+,[0-9]{2}$' THEN 
            CAST(REPLACE(od.auftragswert, ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN od.auftragswert ~ '^[0-9]+\.[0-9]{2}$' THEN 
            CAST(od.auftragswert AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN od.auftragswert ~ '^[0-9]+$' THEN 
            CAST(od.auftragswert AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN od.auftragswert ~ '^-?[0-9]+\.[0-9]{2}$' THEN 
            CAST(od.auftragswert AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        ELSE 
            CASE
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(od.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') ~ '^-?[0-9]+\.[0-9]{3},[0-9]{2}$' THEN 
                    CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(od.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g'), '.', ''), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(od.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g') ~ '^-?[0-9]+,[0-9]{2}$' THEN 
                    CAST(REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(od.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,-]', '', 'g'), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                ELSE NULL
            END
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN od.waehrungscode IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(od.waehrungscode, '[^A-Za-z]', '', 'g')))
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    CASE
        WHEN od.kundennummer IS NOT NULL THEN MD5(od.kundennummer)
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "AccountId",
    od.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z' AS "CreatedDate",
    '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z' AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM opportunity_data od
```
Column notes:
  - Id <- master_opportunities.opp_kennung: MD5 hash of the source opportunity key `opp_kennung` to generate a Salesforce-style Id.
  - Name <- master_opportunities.titel: Trimmed and title-cased version of `titel`.
  - StageName <- master_opportunities.vertriebsphase: Mapped from `vertriebsphase` to the target enum values (case-insensitive). Fallback to NULL for unparseable values.
  - CloseDate <- master_opportunities.zieldatum: Parsed from `zieldatum` (handling DD.MM.YYYY, YYYYMMDD, MM/DD/YYYY, YYYY-MM-DD formats) and output as ISO YYYY-MM-DD. NULL for unparseable.
  - Amount <- master_opportunities.auftragswert: Stripped of currency symbols and locale-specific separators (e.g., EUR 1.234,56 → 1234.56). Cast to DOUBLE PRECISION. NULL for unparseable or 'None'.
  - CurrencyIsoCode <- master_opportunities.waehrungscode: Trimmed and uppercased `waehrungscode` with non-alphabetic characters removed.
  - AccountId <- master_opportunities.kunden_ref, master_kunden.kundennummer: MD5 hash of `kundennummer` from `master_kunden`, joined via `kunden_ref` (with KD- → CUST- prefix replacement). NULL if no match.
  - Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c <- master_opportunities.opp_kennung: Directly mapped from `opp_kennung`.
  - CreatedDate <- (none): Static default timestamp '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z'.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Static default timestamp '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z'.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Default to 0 (false) for all rows.
Reasoning: The model joins `master_opportunities` with `master_kunden` using a prefix-replaced `kunden_ref` (KD- → CUST-) to resolve `AccountId`. It handles multiple date and amount formats, maps `vertriebsphase` to the target enum, and ensures NOT NULL constraints with defaults.

```sql
WITH project_data AS (
    SELECT
        mp."projekt_kennung",
        mp."projektname",

        mp."go_live_datum",
        mp."kunden_kennung",
        mp."opp_kennung_ref",
        mk."kundennummer" AS account_kundennummer,
        mo."opp_kennung" AS opportunity_opp_kennung
    FROM {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_projekte') }} mp
    LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} mk
        ON mp."kunden_kennung" = mk."kundennummer"
    LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} mo
        ON mp."opp_kennung_ref" = mo."opp_kennung"
)

SELECT
    MD5(p."projekt_kennung") AS "Id",
    INITCAP(TRIM(p."projektname")) AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p."projektstatus")) IN ('ACTIVE', 'AKTIV') THEN 'Active'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p."projektstatus")) IN ('ABGESCHLOSSEN', 'COMPLETED') THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p."projektstatus")) IN ('IN PLANUNG', 'PLANUNG', 'IN PLANNING') THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p."projektstatus")) IN ('ON HOLD', 'HOLD') THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p."projektstatus")) IN ('CANCELLED', 'STORNIERT') THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(p."go_live_datum") ~ '^\d{4}\d{2}\d{2}$' THEN
            TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p."go_live_datum", 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN TRIM(p."go_live_datum") ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN
            TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p."go_live_datum", 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN TRIM(p."go_live_datum") ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN
            TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p."go_live_datum", 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN TRIM(p."go_live_datum") ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' AND p."go_live_datum" != '0000-00-00' THEN
            p."go_live_datum"
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    CASE WHEN p.account_kundennummer IS NOT NULL THEN MD5(p.account_kundennummer) ELSE NULL END AS "Account__c",
    CASE WHEN p.opportunity_opp_kennung IS NOT NULL THEN MD5(p.opportunity_opp_kennung) ELSE NULL END AS "Opportunity__c",
    p."projekt_kennung" AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z' AS "CreatedDate",
    '2023-01-01T00:00:00Z' AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM project_data p
```
Column notes:

Reasoning: The model joins `master_projekte` with `master_kunden` and `master_opportunities` to resolve Account and Opportunity references. messages.raw:
ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a meticulous staff data engineer reviewing a dbt mapping proposal.

Your task:
1. Review the dbt SQL models for correctness, PostgreSQL validity, and adherence to the target schema
2. Use the query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect the OUTPUT of the dbt models by querying the output schema
3. Verify data quality: row counts, join correctness, NULL handling, enum values, data types
4. Check for cross-table consistency issues

When querying output tables, quote schema and table names exactly (case-sensitive), e.g. `"my_out_schema"."Account"`.

You have access to:
- The full dbt SQL proposal with column notes and reasoning
- query_postgres(sql) tool to inspect both source and output data

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise (cite specific data issues found via queries)

Do NOT request stylistic changes. Only flag real correctness or data quality problems.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.
